metadata.title: The Model Isn't Broken — It's Honest About What It Learned

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The idea that the panda flip reveals the model was reading a texture channel, not shape, reframed adversarial examples for me entirely. I want to probe which channel my own classifier leans on. Help me: (1) design an experiment that perturbs only high-frequency texture while leaving shape intact; (2) explain how shape-biased training or texture augmentation would change the proxy it learns; (3) describe one diagnostic that tells me whether a deployed model is riding a proxy before an attacker finds it.

props.command: The idea that the panda flip reveals the model was reading a texture channel, not shape, reframed adversarial examples for me entirely. I want to probe which channel my own classifier leans on. Help me: (1) design an experiment that perturbs only high-frequency texture while leaving shape intact; (2) explain how shape-biased training or texture augmentation would change the proxy it learns; (3) describe one diagnostic that tells me whether a deployed model is riding a proxy before an attacker finds it.

beats.narration_text: The model isn't broken — it's honest about what it learned.

card.copy:
The Model Isn't Broken —
It's Honest About What It Learned

beats.narration_text: An image classifier looks at a panda and reports 'panda' with 94% confidence. You add an invisible perturbation — noise imperceptible to the human eye. The classifier now reports 'gibbon' with 99.3% confidence. The panda is still there. It didn't move.
If the image looks identical to human eyes, what exactly changed — and what does the flip tell you about what the model was actually looking at?

props.headline: If the image looks identical to human eyes, what exactly changed…

card.copy: If the image looks identical, what changed — and what does the flip reveal about what the model was actually looking at?

beats.narration_text: The natural reaction is: the model is brittle. Patch it. Add more training data. Smooth the outputs. That reaction misses what actually happened. The model isn't fragile. It learned the wrong thing — and the perturbation reveals the difference.

The panda image has two components. A shape channel — the outlines, pose, and color that humans recognize as 'panda.' And a texture channel — high-frequency statistical patterns invisible to humans. The perturbation left the shape channel unchanged and slid the texture channel from panda-signature to gibbon-signature.
The model was never looking at what you thought it was looking at.

props.headline: The model was never looking at what you thought it was…

beats.image_description: Close-up of digital pixel noise or static pattern in black and white, abstract and grainy, suggesting hidden structure in what looks random

beats.narration_text: The model has learned what we call a proxy — a feature in the input distribution that correlated reliably with the correct label on the training data. The engineers thought the model learned what pandas look like. The model learned the statistical signature of pixel arrangements that occurred in panda training images. On the training set these are indistinguishable. Under adversarial perturbation they fly apart.

The model has learned a proxy — a feature in the input distribution that correlated reliably with the right label on the training data. The engineers thought the model had learned what pandas look like. The model had actually learned the statistical signature of pixel arrangements that occurred in panda training images.

If you diagnose this as fragility, you patch it — harden it against this perturbation, train it on noisy inputs. A slightly different perturbation finds a new proxy. The attack surface moves, but the underlying problem doesn't. If you diagnose it correctly, you interrogate the representation — ask what the model is actually betting on.

Adversarial perturbations are not bugs, they are features — genuinely predictive, statistically real patterns that happen to be imperceptible and meaningless to humans. They reveal what the model actually responds to. They tell you which features the model has bet on.

A 94% accuracy benchmark doesn't tell you the model is robust, or that it learned what you intended it to learn. It tells you the features the model bet on happened to win on the training distribution. The perturbation is the audit.

A skin lesion classifier scores ninety-two percent on the test set. A researcher examines the high-confidence predictions and notices: nearly every malignant case in the training data includes a ruler in the image. Dermatologists photographed suspicious lesions with measurement tools. The model learned 'ruler means cancer.' When tested on images without rulers, accuracy drops to sixty-two percent. The model wasn't wrong — it was honest about what it had learned.
The panda never moved. What the model was actually using to classify it did. Adversarial examples aren't a defect — they're a diagnostic.

props.headline: The panda never moved. What the model was actually using to…

card.copy: The panda never moved. What the model was looking at did.
card.sub: Adversarial examples aren't a defect — they're a diagnostic.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. If you diagnose this as fragility, you patch it — harden it against this perturbation, train it on noisy inputs. A slightly different perturbation finds a new proxy. The attack surface moves, but the underlying problem doesn't. If you diagnose it correctly, you interrogate the representation — ask what the model is actually betting on. Adversarial perturbations are not bugs, they are features — genuinely predictive, statistically real patterns that happen to be imperceptible and meaningless to humans. They reveal what the model actually responds to. They tell you which features the model has bet on.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [The Model Isn't Broken — It's Honest About What It Learned] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
